title: Quickstart
text: By the end of this guide you will:

title: Quickstart
text:
1 · Install Rustimate

macOS (Homebrew)macOS / Linux (curl)Windows


brew install rustimate


curl -fsSL https://rustimate.com/install.sh | bash


scoop install rustimate



Verify:
rustimate --version
# rustimate 0.1.0

title: Quickstart
text:
2. Run the demo
To get started , run the demo command.

rustimate --demo

# use shorthand
rustimate -d

Rustimate Guide


Video


Open your current folder and you should be able to see a mp4 rustimate-demo.mp4

title: Quickstart
text:
3. Help
You can use help command to know more about the arguments available.

rustimate --help

or

rustimate -h

Rustimate Guide
Video

title: Quickstart
text:
4 · Create your first script
Create a file called hello.rsl and add the following snippet.
This script defines a simple scene with a text element.
scene "scene1" {
  mode: presentation
  text  "Hello World"
  text  "Welcome to Rustimate Guide"
}






Note



I use Neovim but you can use any editor of your choice to add the following snippet



Rustimate Guide


Video


To run this .rsl file, run the following command to see the frames and ETA.
Rustimate will now:

Parse the RSL script
Generate frames
Encode the video
Generate firstrsl.mp4 in your current folder







Tip



You can avoid adding .mp4 extension


rustimate -i hello.rs -o firstrsl 


Rustimate Guide


Video




That’s it!
You just created your first Rustimate visualization.
From here, RSL scripts can power code walk through and do much more.
I am excited to see what you create.






Note



See License tiers for what each tier unlocks.

title: Quickstart
text:
Next steps

CLI reference → — full list of flags and arguments
RSL Reference → — everything you can express in RSL

title: Troubleshooting
text:
If you get error saying brew is not available or curl command not found then check if these are installed on your machine
$> whereis brew
# or
$> whereis curl



Preferred way is to install via scoop. To install scoop follow their documentation
$> Set-ExecutionPolicy -ExecutionPolicy RemoteSigned -Scope CurrentUser
$> Invoke-RestMethod -Uri https://get.scoop.sh | Invoke-Expression

title: Troubleshooting
text:
If you get error saying brew is not available or curl command not found then check if these are installed on your machine
$> whereis brew
# or
$> whereis curl

title: Troubleshooting
text:
Preferred way is to install via scoop. To install scoop follow their documentation
$> Set-ExecutionPolicy -ExecutionPolicy RemoteSigned -Scope CurrentUser
$> Invoke-RestMethod -Uri https://get.scoop.sh | Invoke-Expression

title: Troubleshooting
text:
Scoop Error
If you get scoop echecksum error, follow the steps below
$> scoop cache rm rustimate
$> scoop update
You can uninstall the older version via this command
$> scoop uninstall rustimate
Then finally install rustimate again
$> scoop install rustimate

title: Troubleshooting
text:
License not recognised
Verify the license key was added correctly:
rustimate license add license.key
Make sure you are pointing to the correct path of the license.key file received by email. Download the key to your machine, place it the same directory where you will run rustimate from.

title: Troubleshooting
text:
Unexpected output resolution
Resolution limits are tied to your license tier:



Tier
Max resolution




Community
720p


Creator
1080p


Pro
4K



If you are trying to render above your tier’s limit, Rustimate will fall back to the maximum allowed.
You can upgrade the tier at any time Rustimate.

title: Troubleshooting
text:
RSL script not parsing
Common causes:

Missing scene declaration at the top of the file
Indentation inconsistencies in step blocks
Referencing an object before it is declared

title: Troubleshooting
text:
Video not exporting
Make sure the --output flag includes a valid file extension:
rustimate script.rsl --output output.mp4

title: Troubleshooting
text:
Still stuck?

Check the Rustimate website for updates
Pro license holders can reach me at support@qubitai.in

title: RSL reference
text:
RSL (Rustimate Studio Language) is the language used to describe the outcome.
An RSL file defines scenes, objects, timeline events, transitions, and visual properties.
Think of it as a sequence of instructions an, object declarations.






RSL compiler coming in 2027



Native RSL compiler is in development. The current interpreter handles the full feature set described here.

title: RSL reference
text:
Mode - Presentation
In presentation mode, v0.1.0, any sub-components add will use a black background.
scene "intro" {
  mode: presentation
}

Text
While presentation mode renders any sub-components added on it but the best way is to add many text blocks you want.
scene "intro" {
  mode: presentation
  text "Hello world"
  text "Rustimate"
}
You can also add use text like this though I would suggest you use terminal mode which we will discuss below
scene "intro" {
text "fn main() {"
    text "    println!(\"Hello from Rustimate\");"
    text "}"

}


Rustimate Guide


Video




Animation
There are two values used by Animation which are static & typewriter. Static is default value.
scene "intro" {
  mode: presentation
  animation: typewriter
  text "Hello world"
  text "Rustimate"
}


Rustimate Guide


Video




Transition
Transition currently have one value called fade
scene "intro" {
  mode: presentation
  animation: typewriter
  text "Hello world"
  text "Rustimate"
}

scene "fade" {
  transition: fade
  mode: presentation
  text "fade effect"
}


Rustimate Guide


Video

title: RSL reference
text:
Mode - Editor
Rustimate can recreate real developer editor environments as the visual backdrop. It does not rendering the IDE itself. The default theme is monokai
You can use this code to explain part of your code. Editor mode will help
scene “scene2” {
    mode: editor
    editor: neovim
}

title: RSL reference
RSL reference
text:
Rustimate emacs


Video

title: RSL reference
text:
Editor Themes
Themes control the colour system and visual style of the entire scene.
Rustimate v0.1.0 uses three themes for both editors

Monokai (default)
Dracula
Nord

scene “scene2” {
    mode: editor
    editor: emacs/neovim
    theme: nord/dracula/monokai
}






Themes



More themes and additional editor support will be added soon.



Nord Theme
This is how the nord theme would look like in emacs.


Video




Dracula Theme
This is how the dracula theme would look like in Neovim


Video

title: RSL reference
text:
Files
You can render specific lines of a real code file using theme and editor:
scene "Code" {
  mode: editor
  editor: neovim
  animation: typewriter
  file: "main.rs"
}

title: RSL reference
text:
Rustimate Guide


Video

title: RSL reference
text:
Lines
Imagine you want to render only specific lines from the main.rs file. This is where lines are quite helpful
scene "Code" {
  mode: editor
  editor: neovim
  animation: typewriter
  file: "main.rs"
  lines: 5..8
}

title: RSL reference
text:
Rustimate Guide


Video

title: RSL reference
text:
Highlight
What if during your explaination, you want to highlight important or specific lines of code. This is where highlight comes into play.
The below rsl file will help you extract lines from 5 to 8 and highlight line 6. And yes, you can include the animation too!
scene "Code" {
  mode: editor
  animation: typewriter
  editor: neovim
  animation: typewriter
  file: "main.rs"
  lines: 5..8
  highlight: [6]
}

title: RSL reference
text:
Rustimate Guide


Video

title: Rustimate Guide
text:
Introduction
Rustimate is a programmable engine for visual technical story telling. It lets you write scripts and render explanations.
It is designed for:

Technical bloggers
Educators
Researchers
Documentation writers

title: Rustimate Guide
text:
How it works
Rustimate uses a domain-specific language called RSL (Rustimate Studio Language) to describe scenes. It’s engine renders these instructions into visual frames and export to mp4.
You just write a .rsl file and Rustimate takes care of the rendering

title: Rustimate Guide
text:
Features
This version v0.1.0 has following features.

Cross-platform support which includes Mac, Linux and Windows
CLI based workflow
Fully functional Community Edition
Export to 4K format for Pro users
Your data stays locally
Editor tooling available through Tree-Sitter & Language Server Protocol (LSP)








Video

title: Rustimate Guide
text:
Next steps

Install Rustimate →
5-minute Quickstart →
RSL Language Reference →

title: Editor Integration
text: This guide covers the installation of the rustimate Tree-sitter grammar for few editors like Neovim, Helix and emacs.

title: Editor Integration
text: This guide covers the installation of the rustimate Tree-sitter grammar for few editors like Neovim, Helix and emacs.

title: Editor Integration
Editor Integration
text:
Helix
Helix requires explicit grammar fetching and building via the CLI.
File: ~/.config/helix/languages.toml
[[language]]
name = "rustimate"
scope = "source.rsl"
file-types = ["rsl"]
grammar = "rustimate"

[[grammar]]
name = "rustimate"
source = { git = "[https://github.com/rvbug/tree-sitter-rustimate](https://github.com/rvbug/tree-sitter-rustimate)", rev = "main" }
Commands:

hx --grammar fetch
hx --grammar build

title: Editor Integration
text:
Emacs (built-in treesit)
For Emacs 29+, use the built-in treesit library.
File: config.org
(setq treesit-language-source-alist
      '((rustimate "[https://github.com/rvbug/tree-sitter-rustimate](https://github.com/rvbug/tree-sitter-rustimate)")))

;; Install via M-x treesit-install-language-grammar RET rustimate
(add-to-list 'major-mode-remap-alist '(rsl-mode . rustimate-ts-mode))

title: Rustimate Guide
text:
Rustimate Guide is a structured narrative format that tightly integrates:
It allows authors to publish visual technical tutorials where animations and explanations live side by side.

title: Rustimate Guide
text:
Use cases

Technical blog posts
Product documentation
Research explanations
Educational content and courses







Quarto Markdown support — coming next



Native Quarto & Markdown rendering support is on the Rustimate roadmap.

title: CLI Reference
text:
CLI Arguments
Rustimate has various arguments. We will go over them one by one






Tip



You can use short arguments instead of the longer ones


 $ rustimate --help 
  Usage: rustimate [OPTIONS]

  Options:
    -i, --input <FILE>     [default: project.rsl]
    -o, --output <OUTPUT>  [default: output.mp4]
    -f, --format <FORMAT>  [default: desktop]
    -m, --metrics
    -d, --demo
    -h, --help             Print help 
    -V, --version          Print version

Rustimate Guide
Video

title: CLI Reference
text:
Options Explained

Input
This is the input arguments to specify the name of the .rsl file. Rustimate operates on .rsl scripts via the command line.






Important



For version 0.1.0, the input file should be in the current location


rustimate --input hello.rsl

# or

rustimate -i hello.rsl


Output
The default output will be output.mp4 unless specified by output argument. You do not need to specify the extension , it is inferred automatically.
rustimate --input hello.rsl --ouput hello

# or

rustimate -i hello.rsl --output hello






Important



For version 0.1.0, the output file will be created in the current location

title: CLI Reference
text:
Basic usage

Default .rsl file
Rustimate by default look for project.rsl file for rendering
rustimate 


Custom .rsl name
If you want to rustimate process a specific file, you can provide the -i argument
rustimate -input yourownfile.rsl

# or

rustimate -i yourownfile.rsl


Output
By default, the output file is always named as output.mp4 unless you specify with -o argument. You do not have to add the extension
rustimate --input yourownfile.rsl --output yourownfile.mp4

# or avoid the extension

rustimate -i yourownfile.rsl --output yourownfile

title: CLI Reference
text:
Formats

Desktop
This is the default format
rustimate -i format.rsl --output fileformat --format desktop

# or
rustimate -i format.rsl -o fileformat -f desktop


Rustimate Guide - Desktop


Video




Shorts
rustimate -i format.rsl --output fileformat --format shorts

# or
rustimate -i format.rsl -o fileformat -f shorts


Rustimate Guide - Shorts


Video




Square

rustimate -i format.rsl --output fileformat --format square

# or
rustimate -i format.rsl -o fileformat -f square


Rustimate Guide - Square


Video








Note



Resolution limits depend on your license tier. See License for details.









Coming soon




Currently only cpu is supported. GPU acceleration is planned for a future release.

title: CLI Reference
text:
Metrics

Performance History
Rustimate automatically records rendering metrics after each run:



Metric
Description




Date Time
Date & Time when the frames were generated


Scenes
Scenes in the .rsl file


Total frames
Number of frames generated


Resolution
Resolution of the frames


FPS
Frames rendered per second


Render time
Time taken to complete full rendering





Performance Summary



Metric
Description




Current
Current Run (seconds)


Average
Average rendering time (seconds)


Samples
Number of samples available


Record
Shortest time taken









Note



The metrics shows the recent 8 rendering only

title: CLI Reference
text:
Rustimate Guide


Video








Coming soon



Terminal doesn’t yet support colored output and is planned for next release.

title: CLI Reference
text:
Activate License
Activates Rustimate using a purchased license key file.
rustimate license add license.key

Rustimate Guide


Video



See License Activation for more information

title: Project Structure
text: I propose a well-organised Rustimate project which separates scripts, assets, and outputs. Feel free to have your own structure

title: Project Structure
text:
Layout
project/
│
├─ scripts/
│   ├─ intro.rsl
│   └─ binary_search.rsl
│
├─ assets/
│   ├─ images/
│   └─ icons/
│
├─ output/
│   ├─ intro.mp4
│   └─ binary_search.mp4
│
└─ rustimate.toml

title: Project Structure
text:
Folder purposes



Folder / File
Purpose




scripts/
All .rsl source files


assets/
Images, icons, and other static files used by scenes


output/
Rendered video and image exports


rustimate.toml
Project-level configuration (optional)

title: Project Structure
text:
Docs project layout
If you are building a Quarto documentation site alongside Rustimate, the structure may extend to:
docs/
│
├─ _quarto.yml
├─ index.qmd
├─ installation.qmd
├─ cli.qmd
├─ rsl-reference.qmd
├─ quickstart.qmd
├─ examples.qmd
├─ rustimate-guide.qmd
├─ project-structure.qmd
├─ editor-integration.qmd
└─ troubleshooting.qmd

title: License Activation
text:
After you purchase Creator or Pro license, you will receive a license.key file via email within 12 hours.


$> rustimate license add license.key





Video


Rustimate verifies the license locally — no internet connection or login required. Once verified, the appropriate tier is activated.



Hers’s how tiers are setup



Tier
Resolution
Watermark
Commercial Use
Scenes




Community
720p
Yes
No
5


Creator
1080p Full HD
No
Yes
Unlimited


Pro
4K Ultra HD
No
Yes
Unlimited









One-time purchase



Rustimate uses a one-time license model — you own the version you purchase. All v1.x updates are included. Future upgrades (GPU, live preview, etc.) will be offered as optional discounted upgrades for existing license holders.






Rustimate will always (and forever) run locally.
It never require any internet access after installation.
It does not connect any usage data and everything stays in your machine.




If your license is not recognised, verify it was added correctly:
rustimate license add license.key
See Troubleshooting for more.

title: Installation
text: Rustimate supports macOS, Linux, and Windows. It is a lightweight CLI binary — no graphical environment required.

title: Installation
text:
macOS

Homebrewcurl


brew tap rustimate/tap
brew install rustimate


/bin/bash -c "$(curl -fsSL https://raw.githubusercontent.com/rustimate/homebrew-tap/main/uninstall.sh)"

or

curl -fsSL https://raw.githubusercontent.com/rustimate/homebrew-tap/main/uninstall.sh | bash

title: Installation
text:
Linux

/bin/bash -c "$(curl -fsSL https://raw.githubusercontent.com/rustimate/homebrew-tap/main/uninstall.sh)"

or

curl -fsSL https://raw.githubusercontent.com/rustimate/homebrew-tap/main/uninstall.sh | bash
This installs the latest Rustimate binary into your system path.

title: Installation
text:
Windows
scoop install rustimate

title: Installation
text:
Verify installation
After installing, confirm Rustimate is available:
rustimate --version
Expected output:
rustimate 0.1.0

title: Installation
text:
